items.title: OpenAI bans China-linked ChatGPT accounts that amplified US data center electricity price backlash — used AI-generated cartoons to stoke fears over U.S. data center energy costs
items.description: OpenAI says it has banned two clusters of ChatGPT accounts it believes are operating from China, and that used its models for covert influence campaigns targeting U.S. tech and policy debates.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 2 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter OpenAI says it has banned two clusters of ChatGPT accounts it believes are operating from China, which used its models for covert influence campaigns targeting U.S. tech and policy debates, including one called “Data Center Bandwagon,” that produced social media comments and comic strips blaming AI data centers for rising household electricity bills. Whoever was operating the accounts prompted ChatGPT in Simplified Chinese via VPNs, and posed on X as Americans from a range of backgrounds. Yet OpenAI’s full threat report found that the activity generated virtually no authentic engagement.A second cluster, "Tech and Tariffs," generated anti-tariff cartoons under instructions to depict President Trump but never Chinese leader Xi Jinping, and was linked to a network of fake X accounts spreading false claims that ChatGPT user data had been stolen.OpenAI assessed that the Data Center Bandwagon operators were likely a social media team at a private Chinese tech company working for provincial-level government clients. Among other requests, they asked ChatGPT for comic strips about a grid operator's capacity auction prices, drawing on a regional newspaper's reporting, then posted the output on X under hashtags such as #capacityauction, alongside links to legitimate news coverage.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: While no grid operator was named, capacity auction pricing links back to a real, well-documented dispute: PJM Interconnection's independent market monitor has blamed data centers for an "irreversible" 75.5% increase in power costs across the largest U.S. grid region, with wholesale prices near some data center clusters having climbed as much as 267% in five years. Three U.S. senators have demanded answers from Amazon, Google, and Meta over costs passed to residential customers."This was not a case of an influence operation creating a debate," Ben Nimmo, principal investigator at OpenAI, said. OpenAI rated the activity Category One on the Breakout Scale, meaning it stayed on one platform, with no evidence that it reached genuine audiences. You may like AI data center bans are rapidly multiplying across the US Local political revolts threaten to derail US data center projects Kevin O'Leary claims Chinese propaganda is to blame for anti-datacenter sentiment, 'hundreds of millions of dollars' being spent to kill US dominance in AI The Tech and Tariffs cluster poked fun at U.S.-China competition around tariffs, rare earths, AI, 5G, and industrial resilience, and generated bulk comment batches in English, Italian, Japanese, and Traditional Chinese, the latter aimed at audiences in Taiwan. One operator described the accounts as a "water army," a Chinese term for coordinated troll networks, and asked ChatGPT to design a system for scraping and analyzing social media posts from individuals flagged as risks. OpenAI said its model returned generic data storage advice and declined to help with collection. Fake accounts in the same X network repeatedly posted fabricated claims that ChatGPT user data had been compromised, which OpenAI reckons is an attempt to damage its own reputation.In its full threat report, OpenAI compared the campaigns to the 2022 Spamouflage operation, which researchers at ASPI and Mandiant found targeting Lynas Rare Earths, Appia, and USA Rare Earth after Beijing's 14th Five-Year Plan prioritized rare earths. The new activity followed the adoption of the 15th Five-Year Plan recommendations, elevating AI as a strategic industry for China.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
items.author: Luke James
items.titleZh: OpenAI封禁与中国相关的ChatGPT账户，这些账户放大美国数据中心电价反弹——利用AI生成漫画激起对美国数据中心能源成本的担忧
items.descriptionZh:
OpenAI近期封禁了两个来自中国的ChatGPT账号集群，认定其利用AI模型对美国科技与政策辩论实施隐蔽影响力活动。这两个集群分别被命名为“数据中心跟风者”（Data Center Bandwagon）和“技术与关税”（Tech and Tariffs），均通过VPN使用简体中文对ChatGPT进行提示，并在X平台（原Twitter）伪装成不同背景的美国用户发布内容。

“数据中心跟风者”集群主要生产社交媒体评论和连环漫画，指控AI数据中心导致居民电费上涨。其部分漫画内容涉及电网运营商的上网容量拍卖价格，引用了地方报纸的报道，并在X上使用#capacityauction（容量拍卖）等标签发布，同时附上合法新闻链接。OpenAI评估认为，该操作很可能由一家为中国省级政府客户服务的私营科技公司的社交媒体团队执行。尽管未点名具体电网运营商，但内容直指一个真实存在的争议：美国最大电网区域PJM Interconnection的独立市场监测机构已指出，数据中心导致该区域电费成本“不可逆转”地飙升了75.5%，某些数据中心集群附近的批发价格五年内涨幅高达267%。美国三位参议员已就此要求亚马逊、谷歌和Meta就居民用户承担的成本问题作出解释。

“技术与关税”集群则针对中美在关税、稀土、人工智能、5G和工业韧性方面的竞争制作讽刺漫画，指令要求描绘特朗普但绝不出现中国领导人习近平。该集群与一个X上虚假账号网络相关联，这些账号散布关于ChatGPT用户数据泄露的虚假声明。操作者将账号描述为“水军”，并请求ChatGPT设计一个抓取和分析被标记为风险人物的社交媒体帖子的系统——但OpenAI表示其模型仅给出了通用数据存储建议，并拒绝协助数据收集。

OpenAI的完整威胁报告指出，这些活动实际上几乎没有产生任何真实的用户互动。该机构将这一操作评定为“突破量表”的1级，意味着活动仅停留在一个平台上，且无证据表明触达了真实受众。首席调查员本·尼莫（Ben Nimmo）强调：“这不是影响力操作制造辩论的案例。”此外，“技术与关税”集群还生成了英、意、日、繁体中文（针对台湾受众）的大量批量评论。OpenAI认为，散布ChatGPT数据泄露谣言是试图损害其自身声誉。

报告还将此次行动与2022年的“Spamouflage”操作相类比——当时研究者发现该操作针对Lynas稀土、Appia和USA Rare Earth等公司，背景是中国“十四五”规划将稀土列为优先产业。而本次新活动的出现，正值中国“十五五”规划建议将人工智能提升为战略性产业之后。两起案例显示了AI技术如何被用于低成本、高效率地生成大量看似本土化的虚假信息，但其核心缺陷在于缺乏真实的情感共鸣与有机传播。OpenAI的封禁措施也凸显了对抗这类隐蔽操作的技术挑战——模型本身难以识别指令背后的政治意图，只能依赖后续行为模式分析。

items.title: Amazon’s data centers used 2.5 billion gallons of water last year
items.description:
Just after Seattle enacted a one-year data center moratorium that some of Amazon's own employees pushed for, Amazon shared how much water its data centers use, reportedly for the first time. With concerns about water consumption and energy use a focus of new AI data center construction debates, Amazon says its global data center operations consumed 2.5 billion gallons of water in 2025 at a rate of 0.12 liters per kilowatt-hour of electricity, dropping by two percent from its 2024 total even as it expanded operations. 
Amazon also claims it's using water more efficiently than some Big Tech rivals - this graphic in Amazon's report points to M …
Read the full story at The Verge.
items.content: Just after Seattle enacted a one-year data center moratorium that some of Amazon’s own employees pushed for, Amazon shared how much water its data centers use, reportedly for the first time. With concerns about water consumption and energy use a focus of new AI data center construction debates, Amazon says its global data center operations consumed 2.5 billion gallons of water in 2025 at a rate of 0.12 liters per kilowatt-hour of electricity, dropping by two percent from its 2024 total even as it expanded operations.Amazon also claims it’s using water more efficiently than some Big Tech rivals — this graphic in Amazon’s report points to Microsoft, Google, and Meta data showing each using more water per kilowatt-hour than Amazon did over the past few years.Image: AmazonGoogle by far used the most; however, it appears the data cited is focused specifically on Gemini AI datacenters, while Amazon is reporting on all of its operations. However, Amazon’s data doesn’t factor in indirect water usage at the power plants providing electricity for its data centers, or things like water use from new data center construction.Amazon says that “about 90 percent of the time” its data centers use air cooling, and uses evaporative water cooling on “the hottest hours of the hottest days,” while also raising its servers’ tolerances for heat. Amazon claims its data centers are seven times more water-efficient than the industry average, based on an adjusted number from a peer-reviewed research paper released last year.
items.author: Stevie Bonifield
items.titleZh: 亚马逊的数据中心去年用了25亿加仑的水
items.descriptionZh:
西雅图刚刚实施了一项为期一年的数据中心建设暂停令（这项政策甚至得到了部分亚马逊员工的支持），随后亚马逊便首次公开了其数据中心用水量数据。随着人工智能数据中心建设引发对水耗和能耗的广泛关注，亚马逊表示，其全球数据中心运营在2025年共消耗了25亿加仑（约94.6亿升）水，平均每千瓦时电力耗水0.12升，尽管运营规模仍在扩大，但总用水量相比2024年下降了2%。

亚马逊还声称自己的用水效率高于部分大型科技竞争对手——其报告中展示的图表显示，过去几年内微软、谷歌和Meta每千瓦时用水量均高于亚马逊。其中谷歌的用水量遥遥领先，但需注意的是，图表中引用的谷歌数据可能仅针对Gemini AI数据中心，而亚马逊的数据则覆盖所有运营设施。不过，亚马逊的报告并未计入为数据中心供电的电厂产生的间接用水，也未包括新建数据中心施工过程中的用水。

在技术层面，亚马逊表示其数据中心“约90%的时间”采用空气冷却方式，仅在“最热天气里最热的几个时段”使用蒸发水冷系统，同时通过提高服务器对高温的耐受性来减少冷却需求。亚马逊声称，其数据中心的水效比行业平均水平高出七倍——这一结论基于去年发表的一篇同行评审论文中的调整数据。

items.title: AI is set to consume up to 600 billion gallons of water by 2030 — rising energy consumption primarily to blame as data center power demands rise
items.description: Direct cooling data center GPUs uses only a fraction of the water required to keep them running, and with plans for future GPUs and rack systems to be even more power hungry, this problem could make data centers even more of a resource hog.
items.content: Share Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter Data centers consume significant resources, often to the detriment of local communities. But while water use has been on the lips of those fighting back against data center deployments, the sheer scale of their impact wasn't entirely clear until recently.Many new U.S. data centers are being built on drought-prone land, and their cooling systems, which seem to consume gargantuan quantities of water, only represent a small portion of their total water usage by 2050. So, what makes up this overall figure for water usage? Cooling, of course, plays a part, but ongoing energy demands and chip fabrication also make up a larger part of the story.They're consuming how much?!A recent Guardian report on water consumption cited figures from Xylem, a water technology company. That does raise some questions about the accuracy of the report, and led us to dig further into the numbers from other sources. Although they're not quite the same as Xylem's original reporting, they do paint a similar picture.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Quantifying the sheer amount of water data centers use isn't easy. Much of it depends on the specific hardware being used within the facilities, how they're cooled, and what the local grid infrastructure is like. In a recent MostPolicyInitiative report, it highlights that U.S. data centers in 2023 alone consumed 17.4 billion gallons of water. And that's before all the recent gigawatt+ scale data center projects kicked off. By 2028, direct consumption could increase by as much as 73 billion gallons as some of these new facilities come online.But the keyword, there, is direct. The UNU Environmental Cost of AI's Energy Usage report from earlier this year highlighted how global data center electricity consumption required just under a trillion gallons of water in 2025. AI workloads account for around 20% of that, or 200 billion gallons. That equates to around 300,000 Olympic swimming pools. That share is projected to rise to 40%, or by 400 billion gallons, to a total of 600 billion gallons of water by 2030, giving it a global electricity demand in excess of the entire country of Nigeria and using enough water to supply 500 million people in Sub-Saharan Africa. You may like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google under investor pressure to disclose data center water and power use Most new U.S. AI data centers are being built in drought zones Microsoft CEO says new AI data centers use as little water as a restaurant However, with 200 billion gallons of water (and counting) dedicated to AI workloads, how does that compare to other industries?That's still a footnote compared to the or 26.4 trillion gallons used by U.S. agriculture in 2024 (as per USDA), but it is closing in on international oil refining numbers. OPEC produced roughly 86 million barrels of oil a day in 2024, and with a rough conversion of 0.4 barrels of water per barrel of crude, that's in the region of 550 billion gallons a year.There are still industries using much more water than AI, and much more even than all the data centers combined. However, water use is growing dramatically, and it's not really coming from the increased cooling demands. It's coming from power demands and hardware manufacturing.As Americans' feelings towards data centers sour and, in turn, increase their opposition to their development, addressing the growing water needs of these new deployments may be key for developers who want to see their projects reach completion. For residents facing water shortages, droughts, and water contamination, the danger is more existential.Cooling innovationFor data center developers, the main way they've looked to address growing water demand from their facilities is to improve cooling. Moving away from evaporative to closed-loop, direct-to-chip cooling can have a huge effect on the water used in the cooling process. What to read next AI data center project secretly sucked 29 million gallons of water over 15 months before detected by residents complaining about low water pressure AI data centers trigger massive 'irreversible' 76% electricity price spike in largest US region After a $16 billion Stargate AI data center was built despite being voted down, Michigan towns rush to block new buildouts That's a good thing. Indeed, Microsoft's Satya Nadella claimed that the company's newest AI data centers have cooling systems so efficient that they "can operate effectively with zero water consumption." He also compared these mega data centers to single restaurants in terms of annual water consumption.For home PC enthusiasts, Nadella is talking about using the data center equivalent of an AIO watercooler, rather than just letting the hot water evaporate to cool it down. It's easily more efficient in terms of water usage, which is great from that direct-use perspective.Closed-loop cooling is absolutely an important innovation in data center development, and combined with more exotic ideas like fanless liquid coolers and immersion cooling (perhaps even undersea and in-orbit deployments) could see data center cooling use very little water in the future.But it's not the cooling that's the problem: it's the power being generated to run them. And the one downside to closed-loop cooling systems is it uses more power than evaporative cooling systems. As those power demands rise, so does the indirect water usage of these facilities.Power is the issueThe vast majority of indirect water use by data centers by 2050 will be down to power generation, the Xylem study suggests, and if recent generations of GPU development are anything to go by, those power demands are going to be enormous.Nvidia's Ampere generation A100 enterprise GPU had a TDP of 300-400W. An H200 of the Hopper generation has a TDP up to 700W. A Blackwell GB200 GPU can pull as much as 1,200W. The next generation Vera Rubin? That's now up to 2,300W per chip.Once you start scaling these GPUs up to their full racks, the power consumption is extreme. Where traditional data center server racks consumed between 10 and 15 KW, the latest GB300 NVL72 designs could consume upwards of 150KW a piece. Vera Rubin might be more energy efficient, but its racks could consume upwards of 230KW each.Data centers just weren't traditionally designed with this kind of density of energy demands in mind. Powering them alone will be an enormous challenge and require enormous quantities of water to do it.However, other industries also consume far more energy than all data centers, and thus, consume more indirect water usage from power generation. For instance, steel and iron manufacturing, chemicals and petrochemicals, cement and glass manufacturing, and many other industries each use multiple times more power than all data centers combined.Renewables, water recovery, and nuclearFor hyperscalers, the near-term solution to data center power problems has been to use (occasionally law-breaking) mobile methane jet turbines. These aren't too heavy on water consumption, but have their own environmentally damaging effects with heavy carbon emissions.A longer-term solution to this issue, and hopefully the extreme indirect water demands of these facilities, will be a combination of renewables, nuclear power, and water recovery.The Switch Tahoe Reno exascale data center shows how it can be done. It's a 650MW facility built in 2017 that uses 100% renewable solar energy. In Portugal, the SINES DC Start Campus is a 1.2 Gigawatt facility that's partially online and uses 100% renewables, as well as using seawater cooling to offset its water usage. Although many of the newer data center projects are far larger, as the price of solar deployment continues to plummet, it's certainly possible that data centers powered by renewables can be effective and profitable, without consuming such vast quantities of water.Another option is nuclear energy. New, smaller reactor designs are making it possible to get these facilities online faster and with a more modular design. There's even the possibility of repurposing old aircraft carriers and submarines with onboard nuclear reactors to power data center facilities.Data center developers know this is at least one path for the future. That's why they're rushing to secure access to key materials like Uranium.The future of data center power, especially AI data centers, and the water that they require, is almost certainly some mix of renewables and near-site nuclear power.As the mobile methane turbines popping up at data centers have shown, the developers will often just use what they can get their hands on. Perhaps alongside moratoriums and pauses in construction, data center protestors could make sure that if data centers are built in their local area, the developers should also be required to invest in water infrastructure to offset their ever-growing demands, alongside more renewable energy solutions.
items.author: Jon Martindale
items.titleZh: AI到2030年将消耗高达6000亿加仑水——能源消耗上升为元凶，数据中心电力需求激增
items.descriptionZh:
数据中心的水资源消耗问题正逐渐浮出水面，其规模远超大多数人想象。尽管公众对数据中心的关注多集中在冷却系统的用水量上，但实际数据表明，电力生产和硬件制造才是未来水资源消耗的主要来源，尤其是在AI算力爆发式增长的背景下。

---

### 背景与上下文

近年来，美国许多新建数据中心被选址在干旱易发地区。当地社区对水资源短缺的担忧日益加剧，但直到最近，数据中心的真实用水规模才被量化。根据Xylem（一家水技术公司）的数据以及《卫报》的报道，美国数据中心在2023年直接消耗了174亿加仑水，而这一数字尚未计入近期启动的千兆瓦级项目。到2028年，随着新设施投产，直接用水量可能增加至730亿加仑。

但“直接”用水只是冰山一角。联合国大学（UNU）2025年初发布的《AI能源使用的环境成本》报告指出，全球数据中心2025年的电力消耗间接需要近1万亿加仑水（用于发电过程中的冷却、燃料开采等）。其中AI工作负载约占20%，即2000亿加仑，相当于约30万个奥林匹克游泳池的水量。预计到2030年，AI的份额将升至40%，达到6000亿加仑，届时其全球电力需求将超过尼日利亚全国用电量，用水量足以供应撒哈拉以南非洲5亿人口。

---

### 核心技术原理与创新点：冷却系统的演进

传统数据中心常采用蒸发冷却（冷却塔），通过水蒸发带走热量，直接消耗大量水。为了应对水资源压力，冷却技术正在向**闭环液冷**演进，包括直接芯片冷却（direct-to-chip cooling）和整体液冷系统。微软CEO萨提亚·纳德拉声称，其最新AI数据中心已能“在零水耗下运行”，相当于将大型数据中心变成了一个巨型“一体式水冷散热器”——不向大气蒸发水分，而是通过封闭回路将热量传递给冷却液，再经外部散热器或热交换器释放。

这种闭环冷却的直接用水量几乎为零，相比蒸发冷却大幅减少。更前沿的方案还包括无风扇液冷、浸没式冷却，甚至海底和轨道数据中心。这些创新确实能有效降低**直接**用水，但有一个关键副作用：闭环液冷需要额外电力驱动泵和散热系统，其能耗高于蒸发冷却。而额外的电力消耗又会带来**间接**用水——发电过程需要大量水。

---

### 性能参数与对比数据

**GPU功耗的指数级增长是核心驱动力**：  
- Nvidia Ampere A100：TDP 300-400W  
- Hopper H200：TDP 700W  
- Blackwell GB200：TDP 1200W  
- 下一代Vera Rubin：TDP 2300W  

当这些GPU组成完整机架时，功耗剧烈攀升。传统服务器机架功耗约10-15kW，而最新的GB300 NVL72机架设计高达150kW，Vera Rubin机架可能超过230kW。数据中心传统设计从未考虑如此高的功率密度，而发电本身就需要水（火电冷却、核电冷却、水力发电蒸发损失等）。根据Xylem研究，到2050年，数据中心绝大部分间接用水将来自发电。尽管AI数据中心的绝对用水量相比其他行业仍较小：2024年美国农业用水2.64万亿加仑，国际石油精炼年用水约5500亿加仑，但AI用水量正在快速逼近炼油行业，且增速惊人。

---

### 技术影响与应用场景

巨大的水资源消耗正引发社会反对。美国多地居民因数据中心的建成而面临水压低、水资源短缺等问题。例如，某个AI数据中心在15个月内秘密抽取了2900万加仑水，直到居民抱怨水压异常才被发现。公众情绪转向负面，要求开发商必须解决水资源问题。

对于数据中心开发商，解决路径主要有三条：

1. **可再生能源+水回收**：已有成功案例。2017年建成的Switch Tahoe Reno百亿亿次数据中心（650MW），100%使用太阳能供电。葡萄牙SINES DC Start Campus（1.2GW）部分运行，同样100%可再生能源，并利用海水冷却抵消水耗。随着太阳能成本持续下降，这种模式已具备商业可行性。

2. **核电**：新型小型模块化反应堆（SMR）可更快部署，支持模块化建设。甚至有人提出改造退役航母或潜艇的核反应堆来供电。大型云厂商正在抢购铀资源，为未来布局。

3. **短期妥协与社区博弈**：目前部分开发商被迫使用移动甲烷燃气轮机（虽然水耗低但碳排放高）。抗议者和地方政府可以要求：如果数据中心必须在当地建设，开发者必须投资水资源基础设施（如水回收系统、再生水利用），并搭配更多可再生能源。暂停建设和施工禁令已被证明是有效施压手段。

---

### 总结

数据中心的水资源困境本质上是能源问题。冷却创新虽然能解决直接用水，但无法消除间接用水——电力生产才是水源消耗的最终根源。随着AI芯片功耗从数百瓦飙升至两千瓦以上，数据中心的电力需求已远超传统设计范畴。未来的可持续路径必然是可再生能源、核电与水回收的组合。对于社区而言，与其单纯反对建设，不如争取水务基础设施投资和环保附加条件，迫使开发商承担起应有的环境责任。

items.title: Brad Paisley joins fight as zoo's dispute with AI data center escalates, petition tops 330,000 signatures — Nashville weighs sweeping hyperscale ban
items.description: An ongoing fight over a proposed data center sited just 50 yards from Nashville Zoo has escalated further, with the zoo’s land use attorney filing a zoning appeal to overturn permits already approved.
items.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter An ongoing fight over a proposed data center sited just 50 yards from Nashville Zoo has escalated further, with the zoo’s land use attorney filing a zoning appeal to overturn permits already approved for developer DC BLOX. An opposition petition has already passed 331,000 signatures, with Grammy-winning country star Brad Paisley calling the project "an absolute nightmare scenario" in a video posted to Instagram over the weekend.Go deeper with TH Premium: AI and data centers (Image credit: Microsoft)Photonics and high-speed data movement is the next big AI bottleneckThe data center cooling state of playMassive AI data center buildouts are squeezing energy suppliesUltra Ethernet: The data center interconnection of tomorrowThe pressure now shifts to the Metro Planning Commission, which is set to hold a public hearing Thursday, June 11th, on legislation that would ban data centers larger than 500,000 square feet across Davidson County and keep smaller ones at least half a mile from homes, schools, and zoos. The 69,220-square-foot facility at 648 Grassmere Park would draw an estimated 50 MW from the local grid, and the zoo argues its noise and light could disturb vulnerable species, including clouded leopards it is working to conserve.Bill BL2026-1391, filed by District 20 Councilmember Rollin Horton and passed after a first reading earlier this month, will create Nashville's first zoning rules for data centers, according to the local news outlet WKRN. Facilities over 500,000 square feet would be prohibited outright, while those between 100,000 and 500,000 square feet would need Board of Zoning Appeals approval following a public hearing. All data centers would have to run closed-loop cooling systems that don’t return water to the public supply, and smaller facilities would face buffer zones of 100 to 500 feet from homes, daycares, churches, parks, and other data centers.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: It's been reported that the bill will also look to restrict the use of backup generators to emergencies and testing, and require developers to prove Nashville Electric Service has capacity before approval. Governor Bill Lee signed a separate state law last week requiring data centers to cover the cost of grid infrastructure upgrades their facilities need.The DC BLOX project itself falls well under the size that the bill seeks to ban, and the company already claims closed-loop or waterless cooling for the site, but it’s the half-mile zoo buffer that’d be the blocker should it come to pass. The zoo told NBC News that land use attorney Bill Herbert, a former Metro Codes Director, filed the appeal to overturn DC BLOX's approved permits. You may like Nashville Zoo pushes back on 1.6-acre data center build near animal habitats Small Missouri town ousts city council after it approved $6 billion AI data center AI data center bans are rapidly multiplying across the US "As we move forward, we have taken the next step in our fight against the proposed data center," the zoo said in a statement to NBC News, confirming that land use attorney Bill Herbert, a former Metro Codes Director, filed the zoning appeal to overturn DC BLOX's approved permits. A company spokesperson told the outlet the facility “would not be an AI factory placing a burden on local resources” and sits on land that previously hosted a data center, though he couldn't say what the new facility would be used for or whether AI companies would be customers.The standoff in Nashville fits a pattern of opposition to new data centers we’re seeing nationwide. A Gallup survey from last month found that 70% of Americans oppose data centers built near their homes, and Seattle is set to pass a one-year moratorium on new AI data center construction. If Horton's bill passes, Nashville would have some of the strictest siting rules of any major U.S. city, written in direct response to a facility too small for most AI workloads.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.

items.titleZh: 布拉德·佩斯利加入战局：动物园与AI数据中心争端升级，请愿书超33万签名——纳什维尔考虑全面禁止超大规模数据中心
items.descriptionZh:
在纳什维尔动物园与拟建数据中心之间的对抗持续升级。这座计划建于距离动物园仅50码（约45米）处的数据中心，由开发商DC BLOX推进，占地面积69,220平方英尺（约6,430平方米），预计从当地电网获取约50兆瓦（MW）电力。动物园方面委托土地使用律师、前Metro Codes主任Bill Herbert提起分区上诉，试图推翻已获批的建设许可。反对该项目的请愿书已收集超过33.1万个签名，格莱美获奖乡村歌手Brad Paisley在Instagram上发布视频，称该项目是“绝对的噩梦场景”。

这场争议的核心在于纳什维尔市议会正在审议一项新法案——BL2026-1391，由第20区议员Rollin Horton提出，已于本月通过一审。该法案将创建纳什维尔市首个针对数据中心的专门分区法规。根据WKRN的报道，其主要内容包括：
- 全面禁止面积超过50万平方英尺（约46,450平方米）的数据中心；
- 面积在10万至50万平方英尺之间的数据中心，须经过分区上诉委员会的公开听证审批；
- 所有数据中心必须采用闭环冷却系统（closed-loop cooling），不得将水返还公共供水系统；
- 小型数据中心与住宅、日托中心、教堂、公园及其他数据中心之间须保持100至500英尺（约30至150米）的缓冲距离；
- 限制备用发电机仅在紧急情况和测试时使用；
- 要求开发商在获批前证明纳什维尔电力服务公司（Nashville Electric Service）具备足够的电网容量。

此外，田纳西州州长Bill Lee上周已签署一项州法律，要求数据中心承担其所在设施所需的电网基础设施升级费用。

从技术层面看，数据中心的核心基础设施主要包括电力供应和冷却系统。DC BLOX声称其拟建项目将采用闭环或无水冷却技术（waterless cooling），这类技术通过循环利用冷却介质而非消耗新鲜水资源，在减少对环境的影响方面具有优势。但纳什维尔动物园指出，数据中心的噪音和光线仍可能影响园区内包括云豹等脆弱物种的生存与保育工作。项目本身约69,220平方英尺的规模远低于法案拟禁的50万平方英尺门槛，但新法案中要求数据中心距离动物园至少半英里（约800米）的缓冲距离，将成为直接障碍。

这一冲突并非孤立事件。根据盖洛普（Gallup）上月的调查，70%的美国人反对在自家附近建设数据中心。西雅图市正在推进为期一年的新建AI数据中心暂禁令。在全国范围内，AI大规模数据中心建设正在挤压能源供应，光电子学与高速数据移动也被视为AI发展的下一大瓶颈。如果BL2026-1391法案通过，纳什维尔将成为美国主要城市中数据中���选址限制最严格的城市之一，且该法规正是针对像DC BLOX这样的小型设施（通常不承担大规模AI工作负载）引发的争议而制定。

Metro Planning Commission计划于6月11日（周四）召开公开听证会。DC BLOX发言人表示，该设施“不会成为加重当地资源负担的AI工厂”，且所在地此前已建有数据中心；但他无法说明新设施的具体用途，也无法确认是否会服务于AI公司。这场对峙不仅折射出数据中心与社区、生态环境之间的张力，也预示着未来AI基础设施部署中，选址合规与公众接受度将成为愈发关键的变量。

history.title: OpenAI bans China-linked ChatGPT accounts that amplified US data center electricity price backlash — used AI-generated cartoons to stoke fears over U.S. data center energy costs
history.description: OpenAI says it has banned two clusters of ChatGPT accounts it believes are operating from China, and that used its models for covert influence campaigns targeting U.S. tech and policy debates.
history.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 2 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter OpenAI says it has banned two clusters of ChatGPT accounts it believes are operating from China, which used its models for covert influence campaigns targeting U.S. tech and policy debates, including one called “Data Center Bandwagon,” that produced social media comments and comic strips blaming AI data centers for rising household electricity bills. Whoever was operating the accounts prompted ChatGPT in Simplified Chinese via VPNs, and posed on X as Americans from a range of backgrounds. Yet OpenAI’s full threat report found that the activity generated virtually no authentic engagement.A second cluster, "Tech and Tariffs," generated anti-tariff cartoons under instructions to depict President Trump but never Chinese leader Xi Jinping, and was linked to a network of fake X accounts spreading false claims that ChatGPT user data had been stolen.OpenAI assessed that the Data Center Bandwagon operators were likely a social media team at a private Chinese tech company working for provincial-level government clients. Among other requests, they asked ChatGPT for comic strips about a grid operator's capacity auction prices, drawing on a regional newspaper's reporting, then posted the output on X under hashtags such as #capacityauction, alongside links to legitimate news coverage.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: While no grid operator was named, capacity auction pricing links back to a real, well-documented dispute: PJM Interconnection's independent market monitor has blamed data centers for an "irreversible" 75.5% increase in power costs across the largest U.S. grid region, with wholesale prices near some data center clusters having climbed as much as 267% in five years. Three U.S. senators have demanded answers from Amazon, Google, and Meta over costs passed to residential customers."This was not a case of an influence operation creating a debate," Ben Nimmo, principal investigator at OpenAI, said. OpenAI rated the activity Category One on the Breakout Scale, meaning it stayed on one platform, with no evidence that it reached genuine audiences. You may like AI data center bans are rapidly multiplying across the US Local political revolts threaten to derail US data center projects Kevin O'Leary claims Chinese propaganda is to blame for anti-datacenter sentiment, 'hundreds of millions of dollars' being spent to kill US dominance in AI The Tech and Tariffs cluster poked fun at U.S.-China competition around tariffs, rare earths, AI, 5G, and industrial resilience, and generated bulk comment batches in English, Italian, Japanese, and Traditional Chinese, the latter aimed at audiences in Taiwan. One operator described the accounts as a "water army," a Chinese term for coordinated troll networks, and asked ChatGPT to design a system for scraping and analyzing social media posts from individuals flagged as risks. OpenAI said its model returned generic data storage advice and declined to help with collection. Fake accounts in the same X network repeatedly posted fabricated claims that ChatGPT user data had been compromised, which OpenAI reckons is an attempt to damage its own reputation.In its full threat report, OpenAI compared the campaigns to the 2022 Spamouflage operation, which researchers at ASPI and Mandiant found targeting Lynas Rare Earths, Appia, and USA Rare Earth after Beijing's 14th Five-Year Plan prioritized rare earths. The new activity followed the adoption of the 15th Five-Year Plan recommendations, elevating AI as a strategic industry for China.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.
history.author: Luke James
history.titleZh: OpenAI封禁与中国相关的ChatGPT账户，这些账户放大美国数据中心电价反弹——利用AI生成漫画激起对美国数据中心能源成本的担忧
history.descriptionZh:
OpenAI近期封禁了两个来自中国的ChatGPT账号集群，认定其利用AI模型对美国科技与政策辩论实施隐蔽影响力活动。这两个集群分别被命名为“数据中心跟风者”（Data Center Bandwagon）和“技术与关税”（Tech and Tariffs），均通过VPN使用简体中文对ChatGPT进行提示，并在X平台（原Twitter）伪装成不同背景的美国用户发布内容。

“数据中心跟风者”集群主要生产社交媒体评论和连环漫画，指控AI数据中心导致居民电费上涨。其部分漫画内容涉及电网运营商的上网容量拍卖价格，引用了地方报纸的报道，并在X上使用#capacityauction（容量拍卖）等标签发布，同时附上合法新闻链接。OpenAI评估认为，该操作很可能由一家为中国省级政府客户服务的私营科技公司的社交媒体团队执行。尽管未点名具体电网运营商，但内容直指一个真实存在的争议：美国最大电网区域PJM Interconnection的独立市场监测机构已指出，数据中心导致该区域电费成本“不可逆转”地飙升了75.5%，某些数据中心集群附近的批发价格五年内涨幅高达267%。美国三位参议员已就此要求亚马逊、谷歌和Meta就居民用户承担的成本问题作出解释。

“技术与关税”集群则针对中美在关税、稀土、人工智能、5G和工业韧性方面的竞争制作讽刺漫画，指令要求描绘特朗普但绝不出现中国领导人习近平。该集群与一个X上虚假账号网络相关联，这些账号散布关于ChatGPT用户数据泄露的虚假声明。操作者将账号描述为“水军”，并请求ChatGPT设计一个抓取和分析被标记为风险人物的社交媒体帖子的系统——但OpenAI表示其模型仅给出了通用数据存储建议，并拒绝协助数据收集。

OpenAI的完整威胁报告指出，这些活动实际上几乎没有产生任何真实的用户互动。该机构将这一操作评定为“突破量表”的1级，意味着活动仅停留在一个平台上，且无证据表明触达了真实受众。首席调查员本·尼莫（Ben Nimmo）强调：“这不是影响力操作制造辩论的案例。”此外，“技术与关税”集群还生成了英、意、日、繁体中文（针对台湾受众）的大量批量评论。OpenAI认为，散布ChatGPT数据泄露谣言是试图损害其自身声誉。

报告还将此次行动与2022年的“Spamouflage”操作相类比——当时研究者发现该操作针对Lynas稀土、Appia和USA Rare Earth等公司，背景是中国“十四五”规划将稀土列为优先产业。而本次新活动的出现，正值中国“十五五”规划建议将人工智能提升为战略性产业之后。两起案例显示了AI技术如何被用于低成本、高效率地生成大量看似本土化的虚假信息，但其核心缺陷在于缺乏真实的情感共鸣与有机传播。OpenAI的封禁措施也凸显了对抗这类隐蔽操作的技术挑战——模型本身难以识别指令背后的政治意图，只能依赖后续行为模式分析。

history.title: Amazon’s data centers used 2.5 billion gallons of water last year
history.description:
Just after Seattle enacted a one-year data center moratorium that some of Amazon's own employees pushed for, Amazon shared how much water its data centers use, reportedly for the first time. With concerns about water consumption and energy use a focus of new AI data center construction debates, Amazon says its global data center operations consumed 2.5 billion gallons of water in 2025 at a rate of 0.12 liters per kilowatt-hour of electricity, dropping by two percent from its 2024 total even as it expanded operations. 
Amazon also claims it's using water more efficiently than some Big Tech rivals - this graphic in Amazon's report points to M …
Read the full story at The Verge.
history.content: Just after Seattle enacted a one-year data center moratorium that some of Amazon’s own employees pushed for, Amazon shared how much water its data centers use, reportedly for the first time. With concerns about water consumption and energy use a focus of new AI data center construction debates, Amazon says its global data center operations consumed 2.5 billion gallons of water in 2025 at a rate of 0.12 liters per kilowatt-hour of electricity, dropping by two percent from its 2024 total even as it expanded operations.Amazon also claims it’s using water more efficiently than some Big Tech rivals — this graphic in Amazon’s report points to Microsoft, Google, and Meta data showing each using more water per kilowatt-hour than Amazon did over the past few years.Image: AmazonGoogle by far used the most; however, it appears the data cited is focused specifically on Gemini AI datacenters, while Amazon is reporting on all of its operations. However, Amazon’s data doesn’t factor in indirect water usage at the power plants providing electricity for its data centers, or things like water use from new data center construction.Amazon says that “about 90 percent of the time” its data centers use air cooling, and uses evaporative water cooling on “the hottest hours of the hottest days,” while also raising its servers’ tolerances for heat. Amazon claims its data centers are seven times more water-efficient than the industry average, based on an adjusted number from a peer-reviewed research paper released last year.
history.author: Stevie Bonifield
history.titleZh: 亚马逊的数据中心去年用了25亿加仑的水
history.descriptionZh:
西雅图刚刚实施了一项为期一年的数据中心建设暂停令（这项政策甚至得到了部分亚马逊员工的支持），随后亚马逊便首次公开了其数据中心用水量数据。随着人工智能数据中心建设引发对水耗和能耗的广泛关注，亚马逊表示，其全球数据中心运营在2025年共消耗了25亿加仑（约94.6亿升）水，平均每千瓦时电力耗水0.12升，尽管运营规模仍在扩大，但总用水量相比2024年下降了2%。

亚马逊还声称自己的用水效率高于部分大型科技竞争对手——其报告中展示的图表显示，过去几年内微软、谷歌和Meta每千瓦时用水量均高于亚马逊。其中谷歌的用水量遥遥领先，但需注意的是，图表中引用的谷歌数据可能仅针对Gemini AI数据中心，而亚马逊的数据则覆盖所有运营设施。不过，亚马逊的报告并未计入为数据中心供电的电厂产生的间接用水，也未包括新建数据中心施工过程中的用水。

在技术层面，亚马逊表示其数据中心“约90%的时间”采用空气冷却方式，仅在“最热天气里最热的几个时段”使用蒸发水冷系统，同时通过提高服务器对高温的耐受性来减少冷却需求。亚马逊声称，其数据中心的水效比行业平均水平高出七倍——这一结论基于去年发表的一篇同行评审论文中的调整数据。

history.title: AI is set to consume up to 600 billion gallons of water by 2030 — rising energy consumption primarily to blame as data center power demands rise
history.description: Direct cooling data center GPUs uses only a fraction of the water required to keep them running, and with plans for future GPUs and rack systems to be even more power hungry, this problem could make data centers even more of a resource hog.
history.content: Share Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter Data centers consume significant resources, often to the detriment of local communities. But while water use has been on the lips of those fighting back against data center deployments, the sheer scale of their impact wasn't entirely clear until recently.Many new U.S. data centers are being built on drought-prone land, and their cooling systems, which seem to consume gargantuan quantities of water, only represent a small portion of their total water usage by 2050. So, what makes up this overall figure for water usage? Cooling, of course, plays a part, but ongoing energy demands and chip fabrication also make up a larger part of the story.They're consuming how much?!A recent Guardian report on water consumption cited figures from Xylem, a water technology company. That does raise some questions about the accuracy of the report, and led us to dig further into the numbers from other sources. Although they're not quite the same as Xylem's original reporting, they do paint a similar picture.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: Quantifying the sheer amount of water data centers use isn't easy. Much of it depends on the specific hardware being used within the facilities, how they're cooled, and what the local grid infrastructure is like. In a recent MostPolicyInitiative report, it highlights that U.S. data centers in 2023 alone consumed 17.4 billion gallons of water. And that's before all the recent gigawatt+ scale data center projects kicked off. By 2028, direct consumption could increase by as much as 73 billion gallons as some of these new facilities come online.But the keyword, there, is direct. The UNU Environmental Cost of AI's Energy Usage report from earlier this year highlighted how global data center electricity consumption required just under a trillion gallons of water in 2025. AI workloads account for around 20% of that, or 200 billion gallons. That equates to around 300,000 Olympic swimming pools. That share is projected to rise to 40%, or by 400 billion gallons, to a total of 600 billion gallons of water by 2030, giving it a global electricity demand in excess of the entire country of Nigeria and using enough water to supply 500 million people in Sub-Saharan Africa. You may like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google under investor pressure to disclose data center water and power use Most new U.S. AI data centers are being built in drought zones Microsoft CEO says new AI data centers use as little water as a restaurant However, with 200 billion gallons of water (and counting) dedicated to AI workloads, how does that compare to other industries?That's still a footnote compared to the or 26.4 trillion gallons used by U.S. agriculture in 2024 (as per USDA), but it is closing in on international oil refining numbers. OPEC produced roughly 86 million barrels of oil a day in 2024, and with a rough conversion of 0.4 barrels of water per barrel of crude, that's in the region of 550 billion gallons a year.There are still industries using much more water than AI, and much more even than all the data centers combined. However, water use is growing dramatically, and it's not really coming from the increased cooling demands. It's coming from power demands and hardware manufacturing.As Americans' feelings towards data centers sour and, in turn, increase their opposition to their development, addressing the growing water needs of these new deployments may be key for developers who want to see their projects reach completion. For residents facing water shortages, droughts, and water contamination, the danger is more existential.Cooling innovationFor data center developers, the main way they've looked to address growing water demand from their facilities is to improve cooling. Moving away from evaporative to closed-loop, direct-to-chip cooling can have a huge effect on the water used in the cooling process. What to read next AI data center project secretly sucked 29 million gallons of water over 15 months before detected by residents complaining about low water pressure AI data centers trigger massive 'irreversible' 76% electricity price spike in largest US region After a $16 billion Stargate AI data center was built despite being voted down, Michigan towns rush to block new buildouts That's a good thing. Indeed, Microsoft's Satya Nadella claimed that the company's newest AI data centers have cooling systems so efficient that they "can operate effectively with zero water consumption." He also compared these mega data centers to single restaurants in terms of annual water consumption.For home PC enthusiasts, Nadella is talking about using the data center equivalent of an AIO watercooler, rather than just letting the hot water evaporate to cool it down. It's easily more efficient in terms of water usage, which is great from that direct-use perspective.Closed-loop cooling is absolutely an important innovation in data center development, and combined with more exotic ideas like fanless liquid coolers and immersion cooling (perhaps even undersea and in-orbit deployments) could see data center cooling use very little water in the future.But it's not the cooling that's the problem: it's the power being generated to run them. And the one downside to closed-loop cooling systems is it uses more power than evaporative cooling systems. As those power demands rise, so does the indirect water usage of these facilities.Power is the issueThe vast majority of indirect water use by data centers by 2050 will be down to power generation, the Xylem study suggests, and if recent generations of GPU development are anything to go by, those power demands are going to be enormous.Nvidia's Ampere generation A100 enterprise GPU had a TDP of 300-400W. An H200 of the Hopper generation has a TDP up to 700W. A Blackwell GB200 GPU can pull as much as 1,200W. The next generation Vera Rubin? That's now up to 2,300W per chip.Once you start scaling these GPUs up to their full racks, the power consumption is extreme. Where traditional data center server racks consumed between 10 and 15 KW, the latest GB300 NVL72 designs could consume upwards of 150KW a piece. Vera Rubin might be more energy efficient, but its racks could consume upwards of 230KW each.Data centers just weren't traditionally designed with this kind of density of energy demands in mind. Powering them alone will be an enormous challenge and require enormous quantities of water to do it.However, other industries also consume far more energy than all data centers, and thus, consume more indirect water usage from power generation. For instance, steel and iron manufacturing, chemicals and petrochemicals, cement and glass manufacturing, and many other industries each use multiple times more power than all data centers combined.Renewables, water recovery, and nuclearFor hyperscalers, the near-term solution to data center power problems has been to use (occasionally law-breaking) mobile methane jet turbines. These aren't too heavy on water consumption, but have their own environmentally damaging effects with heavy carbon emissions.A longer-term solution to this issue, and hopefully the extreme indirect water demands of these facilities, will be a combination of renewables, nuclear power, and water recovery.The Switch Tahoe Reno exascale data center shows how it can be done. It's a 650MW facility built in 2017 that uses 100% renewable solar energy. In Portugal, the SINES DC Start Campus is a 1.2 Gigawatt facility that's partially online and uses 100% renewables, as well as using seawater cooling to offset its water usage. Although many of the newer data center projects are far larger, as the price of solar deployment continues to plummet, it's certainly possible that data centers powered by renewables can be effective and profitable, without consuming such vast quantities of water.Another option is nuclear energy. New, smaller reactor designs are making it possible to get these facilities online faster and with a more modular design. There's even the possibility of repurposing old aircraft carriers and submarines with onboard nuclear reactors to power data center facilities.Data center developers know this is at least one path for the future. That's why they're rushing to secure access to key materials like Uranium.The future of data center power, especially AI data centers, and the water that they require, is almost certainly some mix of renewables and near-site nuclear power.As the mobile methane turbines popping up at data centers have shown, the developers will often just use what they can get their hands on. Perhaps alongside moratoriums and pauses in construction, data center protestors could make sure that if data centers are built in their local area, the developers should also be required to invest in water infrastructure to offset their ever-growing demands, alongside more renewable energy solutions.
history.author: Jon Martindale
history.titleZh: AI到2030年将消耗高达6000亿加仑水——能源消耗上升为元凶，数据中心电力需求激增
history.descriptionZh:
数据中心的水资源消耗问题正逐渐浮出水面，其规模远超大多数人想象。尽管公众对数据中心的关注多集中在冷却系统的用水量上，但实际数据表明，电力生产和硬件制造才是未来水资源消耗的主要来源，尤其是在AI算力爆发式增长的背景下。

---

### 背景与上下文

近年来，美国许多新建数据中心被选址在干旱易发地区。当地社区对水资源短缺的担忧日益加剧，但直到最近，数据中心的真实用水规模才被量化。根据Xylem（一家水技术公司）的数据以及《卫报》的报道，美国数据中心在2023年直接消耗了174亿加仑水，而这一数字尚未计入近期启动的千兆瓦级项目。到2028年，随着新设施投产，直接用水量可能增加至730亿加仑。

但“直接”用水只是冰山一角。联合国大学（UNU）2025年初发布的《AI能源使用的环境成本》报告指出，全球数据中心2025年的电力消耗间接需要近1万亿加仑水（用于发电过程中的冷却、燃料开采等）。其中AI工作负载约占20%，即2000亿加仑，相当于约30万个奥林匹克游泳池的水量。预计到2030年，AI的份额将升至40%，达到6000亿加仑，届时其全球电力需求将超过尼日利亚全国用电量，用水量足以供应撒哈拉以南非洲5亿人口。

---

### 核心技术原理与创新点：冷却系统的演进

传统数据中心常采用蒸发冷却（冷却塔），通过水蒸发带走热量，直接消耗大量水。为了应对水资源压力，冷却技术正在向**闭环液冷**演进，包括直接芯片冷却（direct-to-chip cooling）和整体液冷系统。微软CEO萨提亚·纳德拉声称，其最新AI数据中心已能“在零水耗下运行”，相当于将大型数据中心变成了一个巨型“一体式水冷散热器”——不向大气蒸发水分，而是通过封闭回路将热量传递给冷却液，再经外部散热器或热交换器释放。

这种闭环冷却的直接用水量几乎为零，相比蒸发冷却大幅减少。更前沿的方案还包括无风扇液冷、浸没式冷却，甚至海底和轨道数据中心。这些创新确实能有效降低**直接**用水，但有一个关键副作用：闭环液冷需要额外电力驱动泵和散热系统，其能耗高于蒸发冷却。而额外的电力消耗又会带来**间接**用水——发电过程需要大量水。

---

### 性能参数与对比数据

**GPU功耗的指数级增长是核心驱动力**：  
- Nvidia Ampere A100：TDP 300-400W  
- Hopper H200：TDP 700W  
- Blackwell GB200：TDP 1200W  
- 下一代Vera Rubin：TDP 2300W  

当这些GPU组成完整机架时，功耗剧烈攀升。传统服务器机架功耗约10-15kW，而最新的GB300 NVL72机架设计高达150kW，Vera Rubin机架可能超过230kW。数据中心传统设计从未考虑如此高的功率密度，而发电本身就需要水（火电冷却、核电冷却、水力发电蒸发损失等）。根据Xylem研究，到2050年，数据中心绝大部分间接用水将来自发电。尽管AI数据中心的绝对用水量相比其他行业仍较小：2024年美国农业用水2.64万亿加仑，国际石油精炼年用水约5500亿加仑，但AI用水量正在快速逼近炼油行业，且增速惊人。

---

### 技术影响与应用场景

巨大的水资源消耗正引发社会反对。美国多地居民因数据中心的建成而面临水压低、水资源短缺等问题。例如，某个AI数据中心在15个月内秘密抽取了2900万加仑水，直到居民抱怨水压异常才被发现。公众情绪转向负面，要求开发商必须解决水资源问题。

对于数据中心开发商，解决路径主要有三条：

1. **可再生能源+水回收**：已有成功案例。2017年建成的Switch Tahoe Reno百亿亿次数据中心（650MW），100%使用太阳能供电。葡萄牙SINES DC Start Campus（1.2GW）部分运行，同样100%可再生能源，并利用海水冷却抵消水耗。随着太阳能成本持续下降，这种模式已具备商业可行性。

2. **核电**：新型小型模块化反应堆（SMR）可更快部署，支持模块化建设。甚至有人提出改造退役航母或潜艇的核反应堆来供电。大型云厂商正在抢购铀资源，为未来布局。

3. **短期妥协与社区博弈**：目前部分开发商被迫使用移动甲烷燃气轮机（虽然水耗低但碳排放高）。抗议者和地方政府可以要求：如果数据中心必须在当地建设，开发者必须投资水资源基础设施（如水回收系统、再生水利用），并搭配更多可再生能源。暂停建设和施工禁令已被证明是有效施压手段。

---

### 总结

数据中心的水资源困境本质上是能源问题。冷却创新虽然能解决直接用水，但无法消除间接用水——电力生产才是水源消耗的最终根源。随着AI芯片功耗从数百瓦飙升至两千瓦以上，数据中心的电力需求已远超传统设计范畴。未来的可持续路径必然是可再生能源、核电与水回收的组合。对于社区而言，与其单纯反对建设，不如争取水务基础设施投资和环保附加条件，迫使开发商承担起应有的环境责任。

history.title: Brad Paisley joins fight as zoo's dispute with AI data center escalates, petition tops 330,000 signatures — Nashville weighs sweeping hyperscale ban
history.description: An ongoing fight over a proposed data center sited just 50 yards from Nashville Zoo has escalated further, with the zoo’s land use attorney filing a zoning appeal to overturn permits already approved.
history.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 0 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Subscribe to our newsletter An ongoing fight over a proposed data center sited just 50 yards from Nashville Zoo has escalated further, with the zoo’s land use attorney filing a zoning appeal to overturn permits already approved for developer DC BLOX. An opposition petition has already passed 331,000 signatures, with Grammy-winning country star Brad Paisley calling the project "an absolute nightmare scenario" in a video posted to Instagram over the weekend.Go deeper with TH Premium: AI and data centers (Image credit: Microsoft)Photonics and high-speed data movement is the next big AI bottleneckThe data center cooling state of playMassive AI data center buildouts are squeezing energy suppliesUltra Ethernet: The data center interconnection of tomorrowThe pressure now shifts to the Metro Planning Commission, which is set to hold a public hearing Thursday, June 11th, on legislation that would ban data centers larger than 500,000 square feet across Davidson County and keep smaller ones at least half a mile from homes, schools, and zoos. The 69,220-square-foot facility at 648 Grassmere Park would draw an estimated 50 MW from the local grid, and the zoo argues its noise and light could disturb vulnerable species, including clouded leopards it is working to conserve.Bill BL2026-1391, filed by District 20 Councilmember Rollin Horton and passed after a first reading earlier this month, will create Nashville's first zoning rules for data centers, according to the local news outlet WKRN. Facilities over 500,000 square feet would be prohibited outright, while those between 100,000 and 500,000 square feet would need Board of Zoning Appeals approval following a public hearing. All data centers would have to run closed-loop cooling systems that don’t return water to the public supply, and smaller facilities would face buffer zones of 100 to 500 feet from homes, daycares, churches, parks, and other data centers.Latest Videos FromWatch full video here: It's been reported that the bill will also look to restrict the use of backup generators to emergencies and testing, and require developers to prove Nashville Electric Service has capacity before approval. Governor Bill Lee signed a separate state law last week requiring data centers to cover the cost of grid infrastructure upgrades their facilities need.The DC BLOX project itself falls well under the size that the bill seeks to ban, and the company already claims closed-loop or waterless cooling for the site, but it’s the half-mile zoo buffer that’d be the blocker should it come to pass. The zoo told NBC News that land use attorney Bill Herbert, a former Metro Codes Director, filed the appeal to overturn DC BLOX's approved permits. You may like Nashville Zoo pushes back on 1.6-acre data center build near animal habitats Small Missouri town ousts city council after it approved $6 billion AI data center AI data center bans are rapidly multiplying across the US "As we move forward, we have taken the next step in our fight against the proposed data center," the zoo said in a statement to NBC News, confirming that land use attorney Bill Herbert, a former Metro Codes Director, filed the zoning appeal to overturn DC BLOX's approved permits. A company spokesperson told the outlet the facility “would not be an AI factory placing a burden on local resources” and sits on land that previously hosted a data center, though he couldn't say what the new facility would be used for or whether AI companies would be customers.The standoff in Nashville fits a pattern of opposition to new data centers we’re seeing nationwide. A Gallup survey from last month found that 70% of Americans oppose data centers built near their homes, and Seattle is set to pass a one-year moratorium on new AI data center construction. If Horton's bill passes, Nashville would have some of the strictest siting rules of any major U.S. city, written in direct response to a facility too small for most AI workloads.Stay On the Cutting Edge: Get the Tom's Hardware NewsletterGet Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox.Contact me with news and offers from other Future brandsReceive email from us on behalf of our trusted partners or sponsors Follow Tom's Hardware on Google News, or add us as a preferred source, to get our latest news, analysis, & reviews in your feeds.

history.titleZh: 布拉德·佩斯利加入战局：动物园与AI数据中心争端升级，请愿书超33万签名——纳什维尔考虑全面禁止超大规模数据中心
history.descriptionZh:
在纳什维尔动物园与拟建数据中心之间的对抗持续升级。这座计划建于距离动物园仅50码（约45米）处的数据中心，由开发商DC BLOX推进，占地面积69,220平方英尺（约6,430平方米），预计从当地电网获取约50兆瓦（MW）电力。动物园方面委托土地使用律师、前Metro Codes主任Bill Herbert提起分区上诉，试图推翻已获批的建设许可。反对该项目的请愿书已收集超过33.1万个签名，格莱美获奖乡村歌手Brad Paisley在Instagram上发布视频，称该项目是“绝对的噩梦场景”。

这场争议的核心在于纳什维尔市议会正在审议一项新法案——BL2026-1391，由第20区议员Rollin Horton提出，已于本月通过一审。该法案将创建纳什维尔市首个针对数据中心的专门分区法规。根据WKRN的报道，其主要内容包括：
- 全面禁止面积超过50万平方英尺（约46,450平方米）的数据中心；
- 面积在10万至50万平方英尺之间的数据中心，须经过分区上诉委员会的公开听证审批；
- 所有数据中心必须采用闭环冷却系统（closed-loop cooling），不得将水返还公共供水系统；
- 小型数据中心与住宅、日托中心、教堂、公园及其他数据中心之间须保持100至500英尺（约30至150米）的缓冲距离；
- 限制备用发电机仅在紧急情况和测试时使用；
- 要求开发商在获批前证明纳什维尔电力服务公司（Nashville Electric Service）具备足够的电网容量。

此外，田纳西州州长Bill Lee上周已签署一项州法律，要求数据中心承担其所在设施所需的电网基础设施升级费用。

从技术层面看，数据中心的核心基础设施主要包括电力供应和冷却系统。DC BLOX声称其拟建项目将采用闭环或无水冷却技术（waterless cooling），这类技术通过循环利用冷却介质而非消耗新鲜水资源，在减少对环境的影响方面具有优势。但纳什维尔动物园指出，数据中心的噪音和光线仍可能影响园区内包括云豹等脆弱物种的生存与保育工作。项目本身约69,220平方英尺的规模远低于法案拟禁的50万平方英尺门槛，但新法案中要求数据中心距离动物园至少半英里（约800米）的缓冲距离，将成为直接障碍。

这一冲突并非孤立事件。根据盖洛普（Gallup）上月的调查，70%的美国人反对在自家附近建设数据中心。西雅图市正在推进为期一年的新建AI数据中心暂禁令。在全国范围内，AI大规模数据中心建设正在挤压能源供应，光电子学与高速数据移动也被视为AI发展的下一大瓶颈。如果BL2026-1391法案通过，纳什维尔将成为美国主要城市中数据中���选址限制最严格的城市之一，且该法规正是针对像DC BLOX这样的小型设施（通常不承担大规模AI工作负载）引发的争议而制定。

Metro Planning Commission计划于6月11日（周四）召开公开听证会。DC BLOX发言人表示，该设施“不会成为加重当地资源负担的AI工厂”，且所在地此前已建有数据中心；但他无法说明新设施的具体用途，也无法确认是否会服务于AI公司。这场对峙不仅折射出数据中心与社区、生态环境之间的张力，也预示着未来AI基础设施部署中，选址合规与公众接受度将成为愈发关键的变量。

history.titleZh: 构建面向AI规模化扩展的生产级光互联机架
history.descriptionZh: 提供所需的带宽密度和效率，以将AI计算集群扩展到1000个加速器。这篇题为“构建面向AI扩展的生产就绪型光学连接机架”的文章首次出现在Semiconductor Engineering上。
